summary: Machine policy for whether users can change the lock-screen and logon image. The local Microsoft ControlPanelDisplay.admx file documents the same Personalization policy key and NoChangingLockScreen value used by the app.

setting.name: Changing the Lock Screen and Logon Image
setting.casual_explanation: Windows normally lets users change the lock-screen and logon background image. This policy decides whether users are allowed to change those images.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable changes on managed devices so the lock-screen and logon appearance stays consistent.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Preventing changes keeps the sign-in visuals standardized, but it also removes personal customization of the lock-screen image.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents NoChangingLockScreen through ADMX_ControlPanelDisplay and local policy files map it to HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Personalization\NoChangingLockScreen. The public documentation clearly describes the enabled policy behavior and the not-configured or disabled baseline behavior, but it does not publish a raw DWORD table, so this record models the documented enforced state plus the default unset state.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Users can still change the lock-screen and logon image because the device is not enforcing the policy.

allowed_values.label: Prevent changing the lock screen and logon image
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is enabled and users are forced to use the default image instead of changing it.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes NoChangingLockScreen = 1, which matches the documented enabled policy state.

windows_defaults.label: Windows machine-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 version 2004 with KB5005101 and later, plus Windows 11 systems exposing ADMX_ControlPanelDisplay

states.rationale: Microsoft documents user customization as available when the policy is disabled or not configured.

windows_defaults.notes: The default behavior allows users to change the lock-screen and logon image.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want users to keep control over their lock-screen image.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the sign-in visuals locked down.
tradeoffs: Users can personalize lock-screen and logon imagery.
Appearance is less standardized across the device.

recommended_profiles.label: Standardized sign-in appearance

states.rationale: Prevent users from changing the lock-screen and logon image.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want a consistent lock-screen and logon appearance across users.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want users to personalize those images.
tradeoffs: Users lose the ability to customize the lock-screen image.

evidence.title: Microsoft ADMX_ControlPanelDisplay Policy CSP
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the lock-screen personalization policies on this page, including NoChangingLockScreen and the baseline behavior when the policy is disabled or not configured.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft ControlPanelDisplay.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps the machine policy to HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Personalization\NoChangingLockScreen.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft ControlPanelDisplay.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the policy prevents users from changing the lock-screen and logon image and forces the default image instead, while users can change it by default.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes NoChangingLockScreen = 1 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: The local Microsoft ControlPanelDisplay.admx file explicitly defines NoChangingLockScreen under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Personalization, and the paired ADML help text documents that enabling the policy prevents users from changing the lock-screen and logon image. The app writes that exact enabled state.

validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file names the same machine policy surface used by the app. The paired ADML help text states that enabling the setting prevents users from changing the lock-screen and logon image and forces the default image.
